1	---
2	ddx:
3	  id: MET-NNN
4	  depends_on: []
5	metric:
6	  schema_version: 1
7	  unit: <unit>                  # ms | bytes | USD | count | ratio | ...
8	  direction: lower-is-better    # lower-is-better | higher-is-better
9	  goal: <number>                # optional aspirational target
10	  budget: <number>              # optional machine-checkable hard line
11	  source: exec                  # exec | external
12	  scope: per-attempt            # per-attempt | per-bead | per-feature | global
13	---
14	# Metric: <Short Name>
15	
16	## What this measures
17	
18	One paragraph describing the observable, the system boundary, and what a
19	reader is meant to infer from a single value. Be specific about what is
20	included and what is excluded.
21	
22	## Why it matters
23	
24	Tie the metric back to the governing artifact(s) listed in `ddx.depends_on`.
25	Explain the decision the metric supports — for example, gating a release,
26	catching a regression, or guiding a tradeoff.
27	
28	## How it is observed
29	
30	Describe the producer of the value:
31	
32	- **`source: exec`** — name the `ddx exec` definition that emits this
33	  metric and the field path it populates (e.g. `result.metric.value`).
34	- **`source: external`** — name the upstream system or report, the
35	  ingestion pathway, and the cadence.
36	
37	## Goal vs budget
38	
39	- **Goal (`<number> <unit>`)** — aspirational target. Documentary only.
40	- **Budget (`<number> <unit>`)** — hard line. When a gate observes this
41	  metric via `ddx.execution.metric.metric_id`, the gate's
42	  `thresholds.ratchet` is authoritative for landing decisions. `ddx doc
43	  validate` warns when budget and the observing ratchet disagree.
44	
45	## History and projection
46	
47	Metric history lives in the generic `exec-runs` collection (see TD-005).
48	Read it via:
49	
50	- `ddx metric history MET-NNN` — observed values in order
51	- `ddx metric show MET-NNN`    — frontmatter, observing gates, partitions
52	- `ddx metric compare MET-NNN <baseline>` — deterministic delta (refuses
53	  to mix units)
54	- `ddx metric trend MET-NNN`   — aggregated series (refuses to mix units)
55	
56	## Notes
57	
58	Optional. Capture caveats: known noise sources, sampling assumptions,
59	unit migrations, or links to follow-on work in the MET v2 backlog.
60

1	# MET-* examples
2	
3	Two reference shapes plugin authors can copy when authoring metric
4	artifacts. Both conform to the v1 schema in FEAT-005 and the runtime
5	contract in TD-005.
6	
7	## Example 1: `source: exec`, goal + budget
8	
9	A metric emitted by a `ddx exec` definition. The metric has both an
10	aspirational goal and a machine-checkable budget. The observing gate's
11	`thresholds.ratchet` remains authoritative for landing decisions.
12	
13	```markdown
14	---
15	ddx:
16	  id: MET-001
17	  depends_on: [FEAT-001, SD-001]
18	metric:
19	  schema_version: 1
20	  unit: ms
21	  direction: lower-is-better
22	  goal: 250
23	  budget: 400
24	  source: exec
25	  scope: per-attempt
26	---
27	# Metric: ddx test wall time
28	
29	## What this measures
30	
31	Wall-clock duration of `cd cli && go test ./...` on the reference
32	runner, measured by the `exec-ddx-test-walltime` execution definition.
33	Excludes build cache warm-up and post-test artifact upload.
34	
35	## Why it matters
36	
37	Test wall time is the inner-loop cost of every contributor change. A
38	budget violation reliably degrades the FEAT-001 contributor experience.
39	
40	## How it is observed
41	
42	`ddx exec` definition `exec-ddx-test-walltime@1` runs the suite and
43	emits `result.metric.value` in milliseconds. History accumulates in the
44	`exec-runs` collection.
45	
46	## Goal vs budget
47	
48	- Goal: 250 ms — aspirational
49	- Budget: 400 ms — gate `thresholds.ratchet` on the observing
50	  definition is authoritative; `ddx doc validate` warns if the two
51	  drift apart.
52	```
53	
54	## Example 2: `source: external`, goal only
55	
56	A metric ingested from an upstream system. No budget is set because the
57	value is informational rather than gating. `goal` documents the target.
58	
59	```markdown
60	---
61	ddx:
62	  id: MET-002
63	  depends_on: [helix.prd]
64	metric:
65	  schema_version: 1
66	  unit: USD
67	  direction: lower-is-better
68	  goal: 0.50
69	  source: external
70	  scope: per-bead
71	---
72	# Metric: cost per closed bead
73	
74	## What this measures
75	
76	Average inference + tooling cost attributable to closing one bead,
77	sourced from the weekly cost report exported into `exec-runs` via the
78	`ingest-cost-report` external pipeline.
79	
80	## Why it matters
81	
82	Tracks the unit economics of agent-driven development against the PRD
83	sustainability target.
84	
85	## How it is observed
86	
87	External ingestion: the weekly cost report writes one row per closed
88	bead into the `exec-runs` collection with
89	`labels: ["artifact:MET-002"]` and `result.metric.value` in USD.
90	
91	## Goal vs budget
92	
93	- Goal: 0.50 USD — aspirational; trend matters more than any single
94	  observation. No budget is set; this metric does not gate landing.
95	```
96	
97	## What to copy
98	
99	- The frontmatter shape is the contract. The prose section names are
100	  recommendations from `template.md`; reorder if a project convention
101	  prefers something else, but keep the section meanings intact.
102	- For `source: exec`, you must wire a `ddx exec` definition that
103	  references the `MET-*` ID (or file a follow-up bead to do so).
104	- For `source: external`, document the ingestion pathway and cadence
105	  in the prose so a reader can verify the rows in `exec-runs`.
106

1	# Prompt: author a MET-* metric artifact
2	
3	You are creating a new metric artifact (`MET-NNN`) under `docs/metrics/`
4	or another configured artifact root. The artifact must comply with the
5	schema defined in FEAT-005 §"Type-specific metadata example: MET" and the
6	runtime contract in TD-005.
7	
8	## Inputs
9	
10	- `<short-name>` — kebab-case slug for the metric (e.g. `ddx-test-walltime`)
11	- `<governing-ids>` — IDs of the artifacts this metric serves (PRD,
12	  feature spec, technical design)
13	- `<observable>` — one-sentence description of what is being measured
14	- `<source>` — `exec` (emitted by a `ddx exec` definition) or `external`
15	
16	## Steps
17	
18	1. Allocate a fresh `MET-NNN` ID (use `ddx doc next-id MET` if available;
19	   otherwise pick the next free integer).
20	2. Copy `library/artifacts/met/template.md` to the destination (typically
21	   `docs/metrics/MET-NNN-<short-name>.md`).
22	3. Fill the frontmatter:
23	   - `ddx.id` → the allocated ID
24	   - `ddx.depends_on` → `<governing-ids>`
25	   - `metric.schema_version` → always `1` for v1
26	   - `metric.unit` → the actual unit string (no synonyms; pick one and
27	     stick with it — mixed units are refused by compare and trend)
28	   - `metric.direction` → `lower-is-better` or `higher-is-better`; must
29	     match the constants the runtime uses
30	   - `metric.goal` and `metric.budget` — set whichever you can defend.
31	     Prefer omitting a field over guessing.
32	   - `metric.source` → matches `<source>`
33	   - `metric.scope` → narrowest level at which the value is meaningful
34	4. Write the prose sections:
35	   - **What this measures** — define the observable and its boundary
36	   - **Why it matters** — link the decision back to `depends_on`
37	   - **How it is observed** — name the producer (`ddx exec` definition
38	     for `source: exec`; upstream system + cadence for `source: external`)
39	   - **Goal vs budget** — restate the values and what enforcement they
40	     get; note that gate `thresholds.ratchet` is authoritative when both
41	     the gate and `metric.budget` are set
42	5. If `source: exec`, ensure (or file a follow-up bead to ensure) a
43	   matching `ddx exec` definition exists with
44	   `ddx.execution.metric.metric_id: MET-NNN`. The producer must be in
45	   place before the metric can collect history.
46	6. Run `ddx doc audit` (or `ddx doc validate`) and fix any new errors
47	   the new artifact introduces. The metric must be reachable from a
48	   root via `depends_on`.
49	
50	## Quality bar
51	
52	- The frontmatter parses, all required `metric:` fields are present,
53	  and `schema_version` is exactly `1`.
54	- `unit` and `direction` are concrete, not placeholders.
55	- The artifact is reachable from a root in the doc graph.
56	- If `source: exec`, the metric ID is referenced by at least one
57	  `ddx exec` definition (or a follow-up bead exists to wire one).
58	- Prose is specific: a future maintainer can tell what is included
59	  in the measurement and what is excluded.
60	
61	## Anti-patterns
62	
63	- Inventing a third `source` value beyond `exec | external`. `derived`
64	  is reserved for v2 and must not appear in v1 artifacts.
65	- Picking a `direction` string that does not match the runtime
66	  constants (`lower-is-better`, `higher-is-better`).
67	- Setting `metric.budget` without coordinating with the observing gate's
68	  `thresholds.ratchet`. They will disagree and `ddx doc validate` will
69	  warn.
70	- Mixing units across history rows by editing `unit` after observations
71	  exist. Retire the metric and create a new one instead.
72

1	---
2	ddx:
3	  id: FEAT-005
4	  depends_on:
5	    - helix.prd
6	    - FEAT-007
7	    - FEAT-018
8	---
9	# Feature: Artifact Convention
10	
11	**ID:** FEAT-005
12	**Status:** In Progress
13	**Priority:** P1
14	**Owner:** DDx Team
15	
16	## Overview
17	
18	An **artifact** is any file carrying DDx **identity** — a structured `id` plus
19	optional graph and provenance metadata. Identity may be embedded (in a
20	markdown file's YAML frontmatter under a `ddx:` block) or supplied via a
21	sidecar `<filename>.ddx.yaml` for non-markdown media (diagrams, wireframes,
22	images, prompts, audio, PDFs, binaries, etc.).
23	
24	The `id` field identifies the artifact; the ID prefix conventionally
25	indicates the type (ADR-001, SD-003, MET-007, FEAT-012, etc.). DDx does
26	not hardcode artifact types — it manages the document graph generically.
27	Types are declared by plugins (see FEAT-018, Surface 6); the conventional
28	prefix table below is the fallback when no installed plugin claims a
29	prefix.
30	
31	This replaces the previous approach of dedicated `ddx adr` and `ddx sd` commands. Those are removed in favor of the generic `ddx doc` commands (FEAT-007).
32	
33	## Definition
34	
35	### Authority rule
36	
37	**Identity present → artifact.** A file is an artifact if and only if DDx can
38	resolve identity for it, by either of two mechanisms:
39	
40	1. **Embedded identity** — markdown files with a `ddx:` block in YAML
41	   frontmatter:
42	
43	   ```yaml
44	   ---
45	   ddx:
46	     id: <PREFIX>-<NNN>
47	     depends_on: [<other-ids>]
48	   ---
49	   # Content
50	   ```
51	
52	2. **Sidecar identity** — any file `<path>/<name>.<ext>` accompanied by
53	   `<path>/<name>.<ext>.ddx.yaml` containing the identity block:
54	
55	   ```yaml
56	   # diagram.excalidraw.ddx.yaml
57	   id: DIAG-007
58	   media_type: application/vnd.excalidraw+json
59	   depends_on: [SD-003]
60	   generated_by:
61	     run: <run-id>
62	     prompt: <input-selector>
63	     source_hash: <sha256>
64	   ```
65	
66	There is no second-class "managed asset" concept. Either a file has identity
67	(and is an artifact), or it does not (and DDx ignores it for graph purposes).
68	
69	DDx uses content-based discovery: it scans for embedded `ddx:` frontmatter
70	in markdown and for sidecar `*.ddx.yaml` files within the project's
71	configured roots, subject to an exclusion list of tool-managed and storage
72	directories (e.g. `.git/`, `.ddx/`, `.ddx/plugins/`, `.claude/`, `.agents/`,
73	and any `worktrees/` subtree). Identity comes from frontmatter, not from
74	file paths, but discovery is bounded so plugin-shipped or agent-scratch
75	copies of canonical docs do not collide with the project's own artifacts.
76	
77	### Common Artifact Types (by convention)
78	
79	| Prefix | Type | Typical Location |
80	|--------|------|-----------------|

25:indicates the type (ADR-001, SD-003, MET-007, FEAT-012, etc.). DDx does
86:| `MET` | Metric definition | `docs/metrics/` |
154:Metric artifacts (`MET-*`) carry a top-level `metric:` block alongside the
162:  id: MET-007
194:derivation schema exists. `MET-*` artifacts are project-local; multi-node
243:       └─ MET-001
349:- A `MET-*` artifact may link to a layer-1 run definition that emits numeric

alignment-reviews

2026-04-22-baseline.json
2026-04-22-baseline.md

140	  id: REF-007
141	  depends_on: [RSCH-003]
142	source_url: https://example.com/article
143	source_author: Jane Doe
144	accessed: 2026-05-01
145	---
146	# Reference: ...
147	```
148	
149	Identity (`id`, `depends_on`, etc.) stays under `ddx:`; type-specific fields
150	like `source_url`, `source_author`, and `accessed` live at the top level.
151	
152	#### Type-specific metadata example: MET
153	
154	Metric artifacts (`MET-*`) carry a top-level `metric:` block alongside the
155	`ddx:` identity block. The block is type-specific metadata that the generic
156	parser passes through inert; metric tooling (`ddx metric`, `ddx doc validate`)
157	keys off it.
158	
159	```yaml
160	---
161	ddx:
162	  id: MET-007
163	  depends_on: [FEAT-014, SD-016]
164	metric:
165	  schema_version: 1            # required; v1 is the only accepted value
166	  unit: ms                     # required; free-form (ms, bytes, USD, count, ...)
167	  direction: lower-is-better   # required; lower-is-better | higher-is-better
168	  goal: 250                    # optional; aspirational target (documentary)
169	  budget: 400                  # optional; machine-checkable hard line
170	  source: exec                 # required; v1 enum: exec | external
171	  scope: per-attempt           # required; per-attempt | per-bead | per-feature | global
172	---
173	# Metric: ...
174	```
175	
176	Field semantics:
177	
178	- `schema_version` — pins the metric block schema. v1 is the only accepted
179	  value; future versions extend rather than replace.
180	- `unit` — free-form unit string. Mixed-unit history is refused by
181	  comparison and trend projections (see TD-005).
182	- `direction` — must match the comparison constants used by the metric
183	  runtime (`lower-is-better`, `higher-is-better`).
184	- `goal` vs `budget` — `goal` is aspirational and documentary; `budget`
185	  is a machine-checkable hard line. When a gate's `thresholds.ratchet`
186	  observes this metric, the gate ratchet is authoritative for landing
187	  decisions; `ddx doc validate` emits a warning when `budget` and the
188	  observing ratchet disagree.
189	- `source` — `exec` for metrics emitted by `ddx exec` runs; `external`
190	  for metrics ingested from outside DDx (e.g. cost reports).
191	- `scope` — declares the granularity at which the value is meaningful.
192	
193	`source: derived` is intentionally out of v1; it returns when a real
194	derivation schema exists. `MET-*` artifacts are project-local; multi-node
195	aggregation is deferred to a future MET v2.
196	
197	> **MET v2 backlog (informational):** derived-source metrics with a real
198	> formula schema, multi-node aggregation semantics, structured acceptance-
199	> criteria comparators, and per-MET dashboard wiring are deferred. None of
200	> these are required by v1; they are tracked here so future work can pick
201	> them up without rediscovering the shape.
202	
203	#### `media_type`
204	
205	Identifies how an artifact's bytes should be interpreted. Examples:
206	
207	- `text/markdown` (default for embedded identity)
208	- `image/png`, `image/svg+xml`
209	- `application/vnd.excalidraw+json`
210	- `application/pdf`
211	- `text/mermaid`
212	- `application/vnd.ddx.prompt+yaml`
213	
214	Web UI rendering, regenerate flows, and tooling key off `media_type` rather
215	than file extension.
216	
217	#### `generated_by`
218	
219	Marks an artifact as the output of a DDx run. Carries the run id, the prompt
220	selector, and the `source_hash` used at generation time. Regeneration is
221	source-hash-driven: when inputs change, the source hash diverges and tooling
222	can offer to regenerate. `generated_by` creates a separate provenance edge in
223	the graph (FEAT-007) — it tracks origin, not authority, and does **not**
224	cascade staleness like `depends_on`.
225	
226	## The Artifact Graph
227	
228	All artifacts in a project — markdown and non-markdown alike — form a
229	**single directed acyclic graph** via `depends_on` edges. This graph is the
230	project's authority structure: it encodes which artifacts govern which.
231	
232	```
233	Product Vision
234	  └─ PRD
235	       ├─ FEAT-001
236	       │    ├─ SD-001
237	       │    │    ├─ TD-001
238	       │    │    │    └─ TP-001
239	       │    │    └─ DIAG-001  (excalidraw, sidecar identity)
240	       │    └─ US-001
241	       ├─ FEAT-002
242	       │    └─ ADR-001
243	       └─ MET-001
244	```
245	
246	Every artifact should be reachable from a root (typically the vision or PRD). Orphaned artifacts — those with no incoming or outgoing edges — are a smell that `ddx doc validate` flags.
247	
248	### Graph Properties
249	
250	- **Single graph per project.** All artifacts with identity participate in one graph regardless of media type. There are no separate "ADR graph," "diagram graph," or "feature graph" partitions.
251	- **Edges are explicit.** Only `depends_on` creates authority edges. `generated_by` creates a parallel provenance edge that is not authority. No implicit edges from directory structure or naming.
252	- **Direction is authority.** A `depends_on` edge from B to A means "A governs B" — if A changes, B may be stale.
253	- **Authority staleness cascades.** If a node is stale, all its `depends_on` descendants are transitively stale.
254	- **Provenance staleness does not cascade.** A `generated_by` source-hash mismatch flags only the generated artifact itself, offering regeneration; it does not propagate.
255	- **Types and media are orthogonal.** The graph doesn't care about artifact types or media. An ADR can depend on a feature spec; a metric can depend on a PRD; a wireframe can depend on a solution design. The graph is type- and media-agnostic.
256	
257	## What DDx Does With Artifacts
258	
259	All via `ddx doc` commands (FEAT-007):

9	# Feature: Process Metrics
10	
11	**ID:** FEAT-016
12	**Status:** Not Started
13	**Priority:** P1
14	**Owner:** DDx Team
15	
16	## Overview
17	
18	DDx tracks process metrics — measures derived from existing stores (beads,
19	agent sessions) that help teams understand the economics and efficiency of
20	their workflow. Unlike FEAT-010 operational metrics (which you *run* against
21	code), process metrics are *computed* from data DDx already collects.
22	
23	## Problem Statement
24	
25	**Current situation:** Bead lifecycle data (creation time, close time, reopen
26	count) and agent session data (token counts, cost, duration) exist in
27	`.ddx/beads.jsonl` and `.ddx/agent-logs/sessions.jsonl`. But there's no way to
28	query aggregate process measures like "how much did this bead cost" or "what's
29	our rework rate this week."
30

input.content:
---
ddx:
  id: MET-001
  depends_on:
    - FEAT-010
    - SD-005
metric:
  schema_version: 1
  unit: ms
  direction: lower-is-better
  goal: 250
  budget: 400
  source: exec
  scope: per-attempt
---
# Metric: ddx test wall time

## What this measures

Wall-clock duration of `cd cli && go test ./...` on the reference runner,
measured by the `exec-ddx-test-walltime` execution definition. Excludes
build cache warm-up and post-test artifact upload. The value is the
wall-time delta between the runner reporting "test phase started" and
"test phase ended"; it does not include the Go build step that precedes
the test phase.

## Why it matters

Test wall time is the inner-loop cost of every contributor change. It
governs how often developers run the full suite locally and how long CI
holds up a merge. A budget violation reliably degrades the FEAT-010
execution feedback loop and the contributor experience SD-005 is
designed to support.

## How it is observed

`source: exec`. The `exec-ddx-test-walltime@1` `ddx exec` definition
runs the suite and emits `result.metric.value` in milliseconds. Each
attempt produces one row in the generic `exec-runs` collection (see
SD-005), tagged with `labels: ["artifact:MET-001"]` so `ddx metric`
queries can isolate it.

Inspect history with:

- `ddx metric history MET-001` — observed values in order
- `ddx metric show MET-001` — frontmatter, observing gates, partitions
- `ddx metric compare MET-001 <baseline>` — deterministic delta
- `ddx metric trend MET-001` — aggregated series

## Goal vs budget

- **Goal: 250 ms** — aspirational target for a healthy inner loop.
  Documentary only; missing goal does not gate landing.
- **Budget: 400 ms** — hard line. When a gate observes this metric via
  `ddx.execution.metric.metric_id`, the gate's `thresholds.ratchet` is
  authoritative for landing decisions. `ddx doc validate` warns when
  this `budget` and the observing ratchet drift apart.

## Notes

This is a canonical reference for `source: exec`. Plugin authors
introducing their own exec-emitted metrics may copy this artifact's
shape verbatim and substitute the unit, direction, observable, and
producer wiring.

data.output: File created successfully at: /tmp/ddx-exec-wt/.execute-bead-wt-ddx-88507ed5-20260503T161949-e7c7f3c2/docs/helix/06-iterate/metrics/MET-001-ddx-test-walltime.md

input.content:
---
ddx:
  id: MET-002
  depends_on:
    - helix.prd
    - FEAT-016
metric:
  schema_version: 1
  unit: USD
  direction: lower-is-better
  goal: 0.50
  source: external
  scope: per-bead
---
# Metric: cost per closed bead

## What this measures

Average inference + tooling cost attributable to closing one bead,
sourced from a weekly cost report exported into the `exec-runs`
collection. The boundary is "all spend tied to attempts that produced
the closing commit"; it excludes spend on attempts against beads that
remain open at report time, and it excludes fixed-cost subscription
overhead that cannot be attributed per-bead.

## Why it matters

Tracks the unit economics of agent-driven development against the PRD
sustainability target. FEAT-016 process metrics aggregate bead
lifecycle data; this metric layers cost attribution on top so a reader
can see whether closing more beads is getting cheaper or more
expensive per unit.

## How it is observed

`source: external`. A weekly ingestion job (`ingest-cost-report`)
reads the upstream billing export, joins it against the bead store
(see FEAT-016), and writes one row per closed bead into the
`exec-runs` collection with:

- `labels: ["artifact:MET-002"]`
- `result.metric.value` in USD
- `result.metric.unit: "USD"`
- `partition` keyed by closing commit + bead ID

Cadence is weekly; backfills are written with the original close-time
timestamp, not ingestion time, so trend queries remain stable.

Inspect history with:

- `ddx metric history MET-002`
- `ddx metric show MET-002`
- `ddx metric trend MET-002`

## Goal vs budget

- **Goal: 0.50 USD** — aspirational target. Trend matters more than
  any single observation; a single noisy week should not drive a
  process change.
- **Budget:** intentionally omitted. This metric is informational
  rather than gating; it does not block landing and no observing gate
  is wired to its ID.

## Notes

This is a canonical reference for `source: external`. The defining
trait is that DDx itself never produces the value — an upstream
system does, and an ingestion pathway carries it into `exec-runs`.
Plugin authors introducing human-supplied or third-party-supplied
metrics may copy this artifact's shape verbatim and substitute the
unit, direction, ingestion pathway, and cadence.
